data.title: Beaten-Up Yeti Stock Looks Like a Buy — Barron's
data.description:
Known for its rugged travel mugs, Yeti is expanding with new products like backpacks and luggage. The stock could deliver solid upside. By Ian Salisbury

When it comes to Yeti Holdings, investors see the water bottle as half-empty. A better bet: Take a swig of the stock, which actually looks half-full.

Founded in Austin, Texas, by two outdoor-loving brothers, Yeti built a brand around adventure and ruggedness, selling $40 insulated mugs and $800 stainless-steel coolers. The company went public in 2018 and surged during the pandemic as hiking and "glamping" took off. By late 2021, shares had zoomed past $100 from an initial offering price of $18.

Today, the glam is gone. Yeti stock has been stuck in the $30s. Competitors like Stanley and Owala started their own water bottle fads. Earnings growth has slowed. And now there's a tariff threat: Yeti has historically manufactured its mugs and other drinkware in China, and those imports were recently hit with 10% tariffs by President Donald Trump. The stock has dropped 39% over the past three years.

Yet the market is missing Yeti's strength. Sales, while slowing in recent years, should start to pick up with some new product lines, overseas expansions, and a lift from investments in a promising category — backpacks and luggage. The tariff threat looks manageable. And the stock's valuation isn't demanding, at 12.8 times 2025 consensus earnings estimates.

"At some point the market is going to realize the stock is way too cheap for what it is," says Jefferies analyst Randal Konik, who sees the stock reaching $55 within a year, up nealry 50% from Tuesday's close of $37.52.

There's nothing wrong with Yeti's financials. Wall Street is forecasting sales of $1.95 billion this year, up 6.5% from 2024, according to consensus estimates. Earnings growth is expected to accelerate from 7% this year to 8.5% in 2026, putting earnings per share at $3.17, up from an estimated $2.92 for 2025.

Yeti's backbone, drinkware, at around $1 billion in 2024 sales, is expected to grow 4.6% this year. Its coolers and equipment line, estimated at $754 million in sales for 2025, is expected to be up 8% this year and 6% in 2026.

Not everything is perfect. On its fourth-quarter earnings call on Feb. 13, the company said it sees signs of "discerning consumer buying, more promotional activity, and heightened competition, particularly in the U.S. market." That might be underselling the problem. Canaccord Genuity analyst Brian McNamara points out that 50% of Dick's Sporting Goods stores reported Stanley as the best-selling brand over the holidays, followed by 41% for Owala and 9% for Yeti. "Owala appears to be this year's Stanley, while Yeti fades into the background in drinkware," he writes.

CEO Matthew Reintjes dismissed the notion that Yeti is falling behind, saying it's focused less on fashion and more on getting its products into the hands of athletes through partnerships with schools and teams such as the Kansas City Current in women's soccer. "We expect drinkware to be a growth driver in all our markets," he told Barron's.

The data back him up. Global travel-mug sales are expected to double to $26 billion by 2033, according to market research from IMarc, suggesting there's room for multiple brands in the space.

Yeti, meanwhile, is finding new ways to grow. It has expanded overseas, where sales grew more than 30% to just under $340 million in 2024. While Yeti doesn't break out sales by country, Canada and Australia are its most established markets. On its recent earnings call, executives said the company has been expanding steadily in the United Kingdom and Germany. In January, Yeti held a Tokyo event to introduce its products to Japan.

International markets are a big opportunity, says Austin Bone, portfolio manager of the Wasatch Small Cap Value fund, which owns the stock. "There are similar brands that have a 40% to 50% revenue contribution from international," he says, compared with about 20% for Yeti.

A bevy of new Yeti goods, including coffee makers and a recently introduced $200 cast iron skillet, could also provide a sales bump. Yet the biggest opportunity might be in luggage, given Yeti's reputation for rugged quality and its outdoorsy vibe. Last year, the company acquired Mystery Ranch, a backpack maker with a small but dedicated following among firefighters and military personnel. In December, a limited-edition Yeti-branded backpack based on a Mystery Ranch design quickly sold out.

"[There's] a real opportunity for the brand to expand, without going too far outside what they are known for," argues William Blair analyst Phillip Blee.

Yeti's strong balance sheet — it had $281 million in net cash at the end of last year — should give it flexibility to continue investing in new markets while handing profits back to shareholders. The company carries minimal debt and boasts pretax operating margins that are holding steady at nearly 17%.

Buybacks could also nudge up earnings per share. Yeti's board has approved up to $450 million in buybacks, after repurchasing $200 million of shares in 2024. At a $200 million annual clip, Yeti would buy back a third of its current market value of $3 billion over the next five years, assuming its stock price holds steady and there's no additional share issuance.

Even without those buybacks, Blee expects earnings to hit $3.25 a share in 2026, slightly above Wall Street's average target. At 15 times earnings, that would put the stock at $49, up 30%.

Tariffs may not be the problem they're made out to be. Yeti began moving drinkware production out of China in 2023, and it expects 80% of U.S. production capacity to be outside of China by the end of 2025. The 10% Chinese tariffs will add just $10 million to Yeti's costs in 2025, according to Chief Financial Officer Mike McMullen. Reintjes expressed optimism that the company will manage through it.

"It's a little bit of a moving unknown," he said. "We're more focused on making sure that we position our supply chain in places that we think meet the business strategy. And then we'll manage the tariff exposure in the business."

Yeti isn't the leaky, beaten-up bottle the market considers it to be. Its coolers and drinkware can survive some hard knocks — and the stock can, too.

Write to Ian Salisbury at ian.salisbury@barrons.com

To subscribe to Barron's, visit http://www.barrons.com/subscribe

data.title: 13D Filings — Barron's
data.description:
These disclosures are from 13Ds filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission. 13Ds are filed within 10 days of an entity's attaining more than 5% in any class of a company's securities. Subsequent changes in holdings or intentions must be reported in amended filings. This material is from Feb. 20 through Feb. 26, 2025. Source: VerityData

Increases in Holdings

International Money Express (IMXI) Voss Capital raised its stake in the money-transfer services company to 2,157,951 shares. Voss Capital did so through the purchase of 207,951 International Money Express shares from Jan. 22 through Feb. 21, 2025, at per share prices ranging from $17.88 to $19.90.

Following the latest purchases, Voss Capital owns 6.9% of International Money Express' outstanding stock. Shares have lost more than a quarter of their value since the beginning of this year.

Decreases in Holdings

GeneDx Holdings (WGS) Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai, or ISMMS, reduced its stake in the health-intelligence company to 1,373,569 shares. ISMMS did so through the sale of 306,447 GeneDx shares from Feb. 19 through Feb. 20, 2025, at per share prices ranging from $101.01 to $108.57.

Following the latest sales, ISMMS continues to own 4.9% of GeneDx Holdings' outstanding stock, placing it below the 5% threshold that requires ISMMS to report further GeneDx purchases or sales. Shares of GeneDx have gained nearly a quarter in value during the year.

Bel Fuse (BELFB) Gamco Investors lowered its stake in the electronic components manufacturer to 155,438 shares. Gamco Investors did so through the sale of 11,900 Bel Fuse shares from Jan. 2 through Feb. 19, 2025, at per share prices ranging from $80.23 to $90.78.

Following the latest sales, Gamco Investors continues to own 7.4% of Bel Fuse's outstanding stock. Shares of Bel Fuse have gained roughly 2.9% since the beginning of 2025.

BJ's Restaurants (BJRI) Fund 1 Investments lowered its stake in the casual restaurant operator to 2,144,584 shares. Fund 1 did so through the sale of 143,400 BJ's Restaurants shares from Feb. 21 through Feb. 24, 2025, at per share prices ranging from $38.27 to $39.63, and then partially offset those sales through the purchase of 40,000 BJ's shares during the same period at $37.76 to $38.32 apiece.

Following the latest activity, Fund 1 Investments continues to own 9.4% of BJ's Restaurants' outstanding stock. Shares of BJ's have gained approximately 3.4% in value since the beginning of 2025.

Civeo (CVEO) M Partners Fund lowered its stake in the workforce housing and accommodation specialist to 679,447 shares. M Partners Fund sold 380,401 Civeo shares from Feb. 18 through Feb. 20, 2025, at per share prices ranging from $25.12 to $27. M Partners Fund acquired an initial stake of 1,059,848 shares from Dec. 9, 2024, through Jan. 31, 2025, at $21.60 to $24.56.

Following the latest sales, M Partners Fund continues to own 4.9% of Civeo's outstanding stock, placing it below the 5% threshold that would require M Partners Fund to report further Civeo sales. Shares of Civeo have gained roughly 19.3% since the beginning of this year.

data.title: Li Auto Inc. February 2025 Delivery Update
data.description:
BEIJING, China, Feb. 28, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Li Auto Inc. (“Li Auto” or the “Company”) (Nasdaq: LI; HKEX: 2015), a leader in China’s new energy vehicle market, today announced that it delivered 26,263 vehicles in February 2025, representing a year-over-year increase of 29.7%. As of February 28, 2025, Li Auto’s cumulative deliveries reached 1,190,062.

In February, the Company rolled out OTA update version 7.1 to all users, further enhancing the capabilities of its autonomous driving and smart space systems. The newly upgraded Li AD Max V13 now delivers smoother performance when navigating complex road conditions, while new features, such as Sentry Mode High-Risk Video Remote Preview and Front Passenger Exit Alert, provide a more reassuring user experience. Additionally, Li Auto unveiled the exterior design of its first battery electric SUV, Li i8, in February. The Company remains committed to managing each product with patience, aiming to inspire happiness for users and their families.

As of February 28, 2025, the Company had 500 retail stores in 150 cities, 488 servicing centers and Li Auto-authorized body and paint shops operating in 224 cities. The Company also had 1,874 super charging stations in operation equipped with 10,008 charging stalls in China.

About Li Auto Inc.

Li Auto Inc. is a leader in China’s new energy vehicle market. The Company designs, develops, manufactures, and sells premium smart electric vehicles. Its mission is: Create a Mobile Home, Create Happiness (创造移动的家,创造幸福的家). Through innovations in product, technology, and business model, the Company provides families with safe, convenient, and comfortable products and services. Li Auto is a pioneer in successfully commercializing extended-range electric vehicles in China. While firmly advancing along this technological route, it builds platforms for battery electric vehicles in parallel. The Company leverages technology to create value for users. It concentrates its in-house development efforts on proprietary range extension systems, innovative electric vehicle technologies, and smart vehicle solutions. The Company started volume production in November 2019. Its current model lineup includes Li MEGA, a high-tech flagship family MPV, Li L9, a six-seat flagship family SUV, Li L8, a six-seat premium family SUV, Li L7, a five-seat flagship family SUV, and Li L6, a five-seat premium family SUV. The Company will continue to expand its product lineup to target a broader user base.

data.description:
Reporter NameSheena Jonathan
RelationshipDirector
TypeSell
Amount$914,873
SEC FilingForm 4

Natera Director Sheena Jonathan sold 5,700 shares of common stock on February 26, 2025, with transaction prices ranging from $155.66 to $162.64, resulting in a total sale amount of $914,873. The sales were conducted both directly and indirectly through Caraluna 1 and Caraluna 2 Trusts. Following these transactions, Jonathan directly owns 260,789 shares and indirectly owns 86,564 shares of Natera.

data.title: Cardano Price Set For $8.11 ATH In 2025 With This Bullish Wave
data.description:
Amid the crypto market downtrend, crypto analyst Master Ananda has provided a bullish outlook for the Cardano price, predicting it can still reach a new all-time high (ATH) of $8.11. The analyst also raised the possibility of ADA reaching $10. 

Cardano Price To Reach New ATH Of $8.11

In a TradingView post, Master Ananda predicted that the Cardano price could rally to a new ATH of $8.11 as it records “massive growth” this year. The analyst affirmed that ADA is officially in bull market territory following the completion of the first wave of the initial bullish breakout. In line with this, he predicts a new wave that could send Cardano to this $8 price target.

Master Ananda further explained that the initial bullish breakout peaked very early in December last year. The analyst noted that such a strong bullish wave will always lead to a correction, which explains why the Cardano price is witnessing a significant crash alongside the broader crypto market. 

The crypto analyst assured that the present correction doesn’t mean much for the market. He further remarked that this is just a period of rest while the players, traders and market participants consolidate all previous gains. Master Ananda added that the market needs to adapt to the previous massive phase of growth. 

Further Analysis Of ADA’s Bull Market Price Action

Master Ananda noted that in a bullish market, a correction will always end in a higher low. According to him, the higher the low of the higher low, the stronger the chart or pair is considered. He gave an example of the ADA/USDT paid which launched the previous bullish wave from a baseline of $0.3000.

The correction higher-low ended at around $0.6000 for the Cardano price, which the analyst noted is 100% higher than the baseline price. Master Ananda remarked that this means that Cardano is super strong. He also revealed that the first peak ended around $1.35, which he claimed is “awesome.”

Furthermore, the crypto analyst noted that from a low of $0.2756 in August 2024, this move adds up to a total growth of around 375%. However, he asserted that there will be more for the Cardano price this time around in this 2025 bull run. Alluding to his accompanying chart, Master Ananda highlighted $2, $4.84, $8.11 and even $10.5 as the new 2025 peak potential. 

Master Ananda stated that he is sharing this Cardano price action analysis now because he believes that ADA is witnessing the establishment of a short-term higher low. The first low happened in early February and now the second is happening. He asserted that the second higher low will launch the next bull market bullish wave. 

At the time of writing, the Cardano price is trading at around $0.59, down over 11% in the last 24 hours, according to data from CoinMarketCap.

data.title: Zelensky says Ukraine still 'ready' for minerals deal with U.S. after Oval Office blowup with Trump
data.description:
By James Rogers and Myra P. Saefong

The agreement is in the spotlight after an explosive White House meeting Friday between the Ukraine and U.S. presidents

Ukraine's trove of rare earths and other critical minerals remains in the spotlight after an extraordinary White House clash between President Donald Trump and his Ukrainian counterpart, Volodymyr Zelensky.

Friday's Oval Office meeting was supposed to be the precursor to the signing of a deal that would include U.S. access to the war-torn nation's valuable natural resources. However, it degenerated into a public row in front of the world's media; a news conference featuring the two leaders was abruptly cancelled, and Zelensky left the White House with the deal unsigned.

"You're not in a good position, you don't have the cards right now," Trump angrily told Zelensky, after the Ukrainian president clashed with Vice President J.D. Vance over the failure of previous diplomatic efforts after Russia invaded and annexed Crimea in 2014.

Trump and Vance both accused Zelensky of being ungrateful for American support in his country's war with Russia. "You got to be more thankful, because, let me tell you, you don't have the cards," Trump told Zelensky. "With us, you have the cards, but without us, you don't have any cards." CBS reported that Zelensky was told to leave the White House following the clash.

Friday's meeting followed a war of words between Zelensky and Trump, whose administration had, at one point, proposed that the U.S. receive $500 billion of profits from Ukraine's rare earths. That proposal does not feature in the text of the agreement between the nations, according to reports.

The deal's text says that the U.S. government supports Ukraine's "efforts to obtain security guarantees," according to reports. Zelensky has repeatedly pushed for such security guarantees.

Speaking to Fox News's Bret Baier on Friday evening after the Oval Office meeting, Zelensky said that Ukraine is "ready" for the minerals deal, which he described as "the step to security guarantees."

Before Friday's events unfolded, Mark Williams, a risk-management expert and finance professor at Boston University's Questrom School of Business, told MarketWatch that the deal appears to be "a lopsided transaction where the U.S. gains billions of dollars' worth of strategic mineral rights without having to provide an explicit security guarantee."

"The mineral deal is more of a payment for billions in previous U.S. military aid received and for not freezing near-term funding," Williams added.

Here are three things to know about the potential deal.

The full extent of Ukraine's mineral wealth is up for debate

While Ukraine undoubtedly has significant mineral wealth, the actual value of these resources has been debated.

The country holds "immense potential as a major global supplier of critical raw materials essential for industries such as defense, high tech, aerospace and green energy," according to the World Economic Forum. "With vast reserves of minerals, Ukraine can significantly contribute to the global supply chain for many or all of them."

Ukraine is a top-10 global supplier of mineral resources, holding around 5% of the world total, according to the World Economic Forum. It has about 20,000 mineral deposits covering 116 types of minerals, but before Russia's full-scale invasion, only 3,055 of those deposits, or 15%, were active, the WEF said. Ukraine holds the largest titanium reserves in Europe, representing 7% of the world's reserves of titanium, which is used in the aerospace, medical, automotive and marine industries.

Related: Ukraine and U.S. agree to framework of economic deal, Ukrainian officials say

Set against this backdrop, Ukraine's mineral wealth has been reported as being over $11 trillion. Just last year, for example, Republican Sen. Lindsey Graham told CBS's Face the Nation that Ukraine is "sitting on $10 to $12 trillion of critical minerals."

"Estimates of Ukraine's mineral wealth seem to be overblown and the deal suggest that U.S. support for Ukraine under Trump is likely to come with string attached," Hamad Hussain and Liam Peach, economists at Capital Economics wrote in note Wednesday.

"People have raised their eyebrows about the scale of these claims," Ian Proud, a foreign-affairs analyst and former British diplomat told MarketWatch. "If it is so valuable, why haven't they benefited from it already?"

Proud also described the U.S. $500 billion proposal as "a totally bogus number," noting that it is more than double Ukraine's GDP, which was just under $179 billion in 2023.

Ukraine would likely see benefits relatively soon from a proposed U.S. deal to invest in its rare earths and other critical minerals, but there's a "long lead time" - potentially two decades - before the U.S. reaps any rewards from the agreement, according to Gracelin Baskaran, director of the Critical Minerals Security Program at the Center for Strategic & International Studies.

The existing mapping of rare earths in Ukraine is 30 to 60 years old and relies on Soviet-era data, Baskaran added. "We don't have a ton of information on whether these deposits are commercially or economically viable to mine."

Ukraine's resources will need to be identified and mapped, she said, adding that the average time required from identifying a resource to actually producing an asset can be up to 18 years. Add in the exploration of that mapping and the building out of the assets, and that timeframe is closer to 20 years.

Ukrainian President Zelensky would be getting a "good deal out of this," said Baskaran. "He doesn't have an updated mapping of what's underground," and would find out what is underground through the process. He's also going to see investments made for rebuilding infrastructure like energy, because exploration mining is "incredibly energy intensive."

For the U.S., Baskaran said that the deal offers a signal that it's "very interested in minerals." However, "given the longevity of the investment you're talking about - 20 years - there's no immediate benefit" to the U.S.

Rare-earth mining presents opportunities, but there are plenty of obstacles

Russia, which launched its full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022, currently controls about 20% of Ukraine's territory. Estimates suggest that around 40% of Ukraine's metal and mineral reserves are in Russian-occupied territories, according to Capital Economics.

However, news reports have said that Russian President Vladimir Putin is ready to offer access to mineral resources in Russian-occupied territories in Ukraine.

Former British diplomat Proud also pointed to the latest data from the Institute for the Study of War, which shows a significant proportion of Ukraine's rare earths, as well as lithium and uranium, within territory controlled by Ukraine.

"In terms of prospects, the stuff is there," he said. "Any access to it depends on there being a ceasefire - let's assume that there's a ceasefire in place and it holds, there would be access to it."

Related: Oil prices find little support from the first drop in U.S. supplies in 5 weeks

But the question, according to Proud, is how quickly a company could get a return on mining these materials. The analyst noted that Ukrainian authorities would likely be "bending over backwards" to accommodate any Western companies looking to access the minerals.

Rare-earths mining can also be a challenging business, as evidenced by the demise of Colorado-based miner Molycorp Inc., which filed for bankruptcy protection in 2015 amid falling prices for rare earths.

Ukraine could definitely help meet many of the needs for minerals such as graphite, which is essential for electrodes in batteries and electrical devices, but it is unclear what the conditions of these mines are after the Russian conflict, said Rob Handfield, a distinguished professor of supply-chain management at North Carolina State University. "Many of the roads and logistics channels for bringing the product to market may have been damaged."

Ukraine currently produces some critical minerals like gallium, graphite, and titanium, which generate less than $200 million in annual export revenues, but the "reality is that most of Ukraine's mineral reserves are unlikely to be economically viable, especially in the near term," said Hussain and Peach from Capital Economics.

The economists said that that difficulty and high costs have prevented the development of the Novopoltavske field in Ukraine, which is said to contain rare earths and uranium among other critical minerals, even though the field was discovered over 50 years ago.

Related: Zelensky indicates progress on reaching deal with the U.S. on rare minerals

"Greater access to U.S. technology and funding could make it more worthwhile to exploit a handful of deposits," the Capital Economics economists said. "Crucially, though, if the economics of mine development remain unfavorable, most estimated reserves will probably remain in the ground."

Steve Hanke, a professor of applied economics at Johns Hopkins University, who was responsible for the natural-resources portfolio when he served on President Ronald Reagan's Council of Economic Advisors, told MarketWatch that as far as any deal with Ukraine is concerned, "it is very problematic."

"Ukraine is very corrupt," Hanke added. In a report released last month, Ukraine's National Agency on Corruption Prevention said "corruption continues to be the second-biggest problem in Ukraine after Russia's armed aggression." However, the agency noted that Ukrainians have become more active in reporting corruption.

The implications of the U.S.-Ukraine minerals deal are wide-ranging

Washington's bold move to tap into Ukraine's mineral wealth reflects ongoing U.S. competition with China, according to British diplomat Proud.

"China is so significant when it comes to rare earths," he told MarketWatch. "They were looking to invest in Ukraine before the Ukraine crisis bubbled up - getting the U.S. in there before China and the Europeans is a big part of this."

"The U.S. has been doing little to nothing in reducing dependence on China for these minerals," said North Carolina State's Handfield. China would also "be likely to swoop into Ukraine and invest in these mines if the U.S. does not."

Related: As hopes grow for a cease-fire in Ukraine, J.P. Morgan looks at the impact on stocks

Johns Hopkins' Hanke said that the U.S. is so far behind China in what he referred to as the "3 M's" - mining, metallurgy and material science - that it will "take further than the eye can see for the U.S. to catch up."

"China simply dominates the critical-materials space and will continue to do so for the foreseeable future," he said.

Furthermore, overall "terms of the agreement are still vague, and light on details whether U.S. mining companies will have access or Ukraine mining companies will drive the extraction and supply arrangement," Boston University's Williams said.

The economic benefits to the U.S. are clear. "Securing access to raw materials will be a key priority for the U.S. and its allies as the global economy continues to fracture," said Hussain and Peach from Capital Economics.

But the deal also brings risks, according to Williams. "There are a few elements of this transaction which are alarming," he told MarketWatch.

"This transaction sets a negative precedence," he added. "It is structured with [the] U.S. as business opportunist instead of world leader and protector of sovereign rights." Williams questioned whether the U.S. government is now an "opportunistic hedge fund seeking to exploit other countries' resources."

Trump is "using the threat of withholding U.S. defense support and Ukraine vulnerability as a means to gain ownership of long-term critical minerals. This puts 'art of the deal' tactics ahead of world leader and defender role so central to U.S. identity," he said.

Related: Ukraine bonds rally - will they become a proxy for peace hopes?

"Even if a deal is struck for billions of dollars' worth of critical minerals, will Ukraine have the means and desire to honor this contract indefinitely?" Williams said. "Given mine locations, and level of war destruction, limited resources need to be used to rebuild post-war Ukraine and not for extracting minerals for the U.S."

-James Rogers -Myra P. Saefong

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.
